coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence sensing and effective elimination of Listeria monocytogenes in food based on Janus gold carbon dots-silver nanoclusters composites

coredata.dc:creator: Ling, Zhiming
Xu, Qian
Li, Weiqiang
Gao, Jiatian
Xu, Hengyi
coredata.dc:description: This study developed a method for sensitive detection and effective elimination of Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) in food products. L. monocytogenes is one of the most threatening foodborne pathogens around the world, posing a serious threat to food safety and public health. Therefore, the detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes is significant for ensuring food safety and preventing secondary contamination. Herein, a bifunctional gold carbon dots-silver nanoclusters (GCDs-AgNCs) nanocomposite with excellent fluorescence properties and antimicrobial activity was synthesized in this study. On the one hand, a fluorescence biosensor for the detection of L. monocytogenes by sandwich method was constructed with magnetic separation technique and aptamer-modified GCDs-AgNCs. The biosensor exhibited great detection performance for L. monocytogenes in spiked samples with a limit of detection of 160 CFU/mL. On the other hand, GCDs-AgNCs with antimicrobial activity can eliminate L. monocytogenes by destroying the bacterial cell structure and affecting bacterial metabolism, which effectively avoids secondary contamination. Therefore, this bifunctional nanomaterial can provide a referable new strategy for the rapid analysis and effective elimination of foodborne pathogens.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Janus GCDs-AgNCs
Magnetic separation
Fluorescence biosensor
Bacteria elimination
L. monocytogenes

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Procedure for detecting L. monocytogenes in spiked food samples
Antimicrobial assay of prepared nanomaterials against L. monocytogenes
Principle of fluorescence detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes
Antimicrobial activity and mechanism of prepared AgNCs for L. monocytogenes
Optimization of detection procedure parameters for the proposed fluorescence detection strategy
Capability and stability of MNPs-Amx to separate L. monocytogenes
Specificity of the constructed fluorescent biosensor
Performance of the constructed fluorescence biosensor for detecting L. monocytogenes
Evaluation of the antimicrobial effect of GCDs-AgNCs against L. monocytogenes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We are grateful to the Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University (Project No. SKLF-ZZB-202328) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of MNPs-Amx. (A) Hydrodynamic diameter and Zeta potential of MNPs and MNPs-Amx. (B) FT-IR spectra of MNPs and MNPs-Amx. (C) SEM images of MNPs, MNPs-Amx, L. monocytogenes, and MNPs-Amx ∼ L. monocytogenes.

The characterization of GCDs. (A) TEM image of GCDs. (B) High-resolution TEM image of GCDs. (C) Particle size distribution of GCDs. (D) XPS full spectra of GCDs. (E–H) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and Au 4f of GCDs. (I) UV-vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of GCDs (Inset: images of GCDs solution under natural and UV light). (J) Fluorescence emission spectra of GCDs in different excitation wavelengths; (K) Fluorescent stability of GCDs at 365 nm UV light. (L) Fluorescence decay curve (dot plot) and fitting curve (solid line) of GCDs.

Antimicrobial activity of AgNCs against L. monocytogenes. (A) Determination of the minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) of AgNCs; (B) Determination of the bactericidal time of AgNCs; (C) Plate coating results of L. monocytogenes after treatment with different concentrations of AgNCs.

Optimization of parameters for magnetic separation and detection procedure. (A) The amounts of MNPs-Amx. (B) The incubation time of MNPs-Amx with bacteria. (C) Magnetic separation time of MNPs-Amx ∼ L. monocytogenes. (D) The dosage of Apt-GCDs-AgNCs. (E) Labeling time of Apt-GCDs-AgNCs. (F) Schematic representation of the fluorescence detection strategy.

Specificity of the constructed fluorescence biosensor. Fluorescence spectra (A) and fluorescence signal at 625 nm (B) by using the proposed sandwich assay. 1: Control, 2: E. coli O157:H7 ATCC43888, 3: S. flexneri CMCC51572, 4: S. typhimurium ATCC13311, 5: B. cereus JX-CDC JDZ0102Y, 6: S. aureus CMCC26003, 7: L. monocytogenes ATCC13932, 8: mixture of L. monocytogenes ATCC13932, E. coli O157:H7 ATCC43888 and S. aureus CMCC26003. The concentrations of bacteria were 105 CFU/mL.

Sensitivity of the proposed fluorescence biosensor in spiked samples. Fluorescence spectra (A) and calibration curve (D) for the detection of L. monocytogenes at different concentrations (1.6 × 101–1.6 × 106 CFU/mL) in PBS samples. Fluorescence spectra (B) and calibration curve (E) for the detection of L. monocytogenes at different concentrations (1.6 × 101–1.6 × 106 CFU/mL) in spiked lettuce samples. Fluorescence spectra (C) and calibration curve (F) for the detection of L. monocytogenes at different concentrations (1.6 × 101–1.6 × 106 CFU/mL) in spiked juice samples. The cut-off value (dash lines): mean+3SD of the control group.

Schematic illustration of fluorescence sensing and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes. (A) Preparation of MNPs-Amx. (B) Preparation of GCDs-AgNCs. (C) Principles of fluorescence detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence sensing and effective elimination of Listeria monocytogenes in food based on Janus gold carbon dots-silver nanoclusters composites

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330047, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: International Institute of Food Innovation Co., Ltd., Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330200, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University. 235 Nanjing East Road, Nanchang 330047, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study developed a method for sensitive detection and effective elimination of Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) in food products. L. monocytogenes is one of the most threatening foodborne pathogens around the world, posing a serious threat to food safety and public health. Therefore, the detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes is significant for ensuring food safety and preventing secondary contamination. Herein, a bifunctional gold carbon dots-silver nanoclusters (GCDs-AgNCs) nanocomposite with excellent fluorescence properties and antimicrobial activity was synthesized in this study. On the one hand, a fluorescence biosensor for the detection of L. monocytogenes by sandwich method was constructed with magnetic separation technique and aptamer-modified GCDs-AgNCs. The biosensor exhibited great detection performance for L. monocytogenes in spiked samples with a limit of detection of 160 CFU/mL. On the other hand, GCDs-AgNCs with antimicrobial activity can eliminate L. monocytogenes by destroying the bacterial cell structure and affecting bacterial metabolism, which effectively avoids secondary contamination. Therefore, this bifunctional nanomaterial can provide a referable new strategy for the rapid analysis and effective elimination of foodborne pathogens.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Listeria monocytogenes (L. monocytogenes) is the most pathogenic foodborne pathogen in the Listeria genus, which is broadly present in contaminated vegetables, meat products, milk, and many prepared foods (Su et al., 2024). Due to the ability to grow and reproduce at 4 °C, L. monocytogenes is also known as the “refrigerator killer”. L. monocytogenes infection can cause various illnesses, ranging from the common diarrhea to the dangerous sepsis and meningitis (Bai et al., 2023). Reportedly, L. monocytogenes infection is particularly affected in susceptible populations with weak immune systems, such as newborns, pregnant women, and the elderly (Feng et al., 2024). Therefore, developing accurate and sensitive detection methods and effective elimination methods for L. monocytogenes is significant for ensuring food safety. Currently, the common screening methods for pathogens include the gold standard culture method, immunological method, and molecular biology method based on nucleic acid amplification (Li et al., 2025; Zhang et al., 2016). However, these methods have non-negligible limitations such as long time-consuming, complicated operation and potential false-positive and false-negative results, which has promoted the development of novel detection methods for pathogens (Nnachi et al., 2022).

Nowadays, with the rapid development of nanotechnology, many nanomaterials with excellent physicochemical properties have been discovered or manufactured, such as gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (Kaushal et al., 2021), quantum dots (QDs) (Zhang et al., 2023), carbon dots (CDs) (Fu et al., 2022), metal-organic frameworks (MOF) (Huang et al., 2023), and so on. Among these nanomaterials, CDs have received extensive attention from researchers because of simple synthesis pathway, low toxicity and good biocompatibility (Xu et al., 2023). Furthermore, CDs typically have excellent fluorescence properties, good modifiability, great dispersibility, and photo-bleaching resistance. These unique properties provide CDs with significant advantages in the field of biomarking and sensing, which are widely used to construct fluorescence biosensors for pathogen detection (Liu et al., 2024). These fluorescence biosensors are capable of converting the amount of the bacteria into measurable physicochemical signals to rapidly obtain detection results (Ma et al., 2022). And compared with other type of biosensors, CDs-based fluorescence biosensors are simple to operate, high sensitive, and responsive (Yan, Chen, et al., 2022).

Moreover, due to their small particle size and abundant surface functional groups, CDs are capable of binding with porous materials such as silica nanocapsules (Yang et al., 2018), covalent organic frameworks (COF) (Yang et al., 2024) and MOF (Peng et al., 2024) to amplify the fluorescence detection signals. Previously, we encapsulated red fluorescent CDs in spore-like silica nanocapsules, which increased 2.9-fold fluorescence signal through the encapsulation and release of CDs and achieved highly sensitive detection of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) with the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 25 CFU/mL (Ling et al., 2024). Besides, CDs were also conveniently conjugated with many functional molecules, metal nanoclusters, and metal oxides to obtain CDs composites with multiple functionalities to broaden the application field of CDs. Jijie et al., synthesized CDs-Amp by coupling CDs with ampicillin (Amp) with the carbodiimide method, which conferred excellent antimicrobial activity to CDs (Jijie et al., 2018). Liu et al., coupled CDs with AuAg nanoclusters (AuAgNCs) to synthesize CDs@AuAgNCs composites with better visible light capture and charge transfer capabilities, which endowed the composites with excellent photodynamic antimicrobial activity reducing bacterial with 4–5 orders of magnitude (Liu et al., 2022). Therefore, binding CDs to silver nanoclusters (AgNCs) with antimicrobial activity should confer antimicrobial function to CDs and effectively eliminate foodborne pathogens (Wang et al., 2022).

Herein, Amx-functionalized magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs-Amx) were synthesized for enriching L. monocytogenes based on the recognition of bacterial penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) by amoxicillin (Amx) (Bai et al., 2022). Afterward, gold-doped carbon dots (GCDs) with orange-red fluorescence and AgNCs with antimicrobial activity were used to prepare bifunctional composites (GCDs-AgNCs) as detection probe, which realized the sensitive detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes. The advantages of the constructed strategy were as follows: MNPs-Amx have high affinity against L. monocytogenes, which enabled the enrichment of more than 84.5% of L. monocytogenes in spiked juice samples. Further, GCDs and AgNCs require only simple synthetic pathways and moderate synthesis conditions. Lastly, GCDs-AgNCs have both excellent fluorescence properties and antimicrobial activity, which can realize the sensitive detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes in food samples.
The materials and reagents, synthesis and modification of nanomaterials, antimicrobial experiments and procedure for separation of L. monocytogenes were described in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for detecting L. monocytogenes in spiked food samples
ce:section.ce:para: Experiments for the detection of L. monocytogenes in spiked lettuce and fruit juices were performed to evaluate the feasibility of the constructed strategy. Typically, 40 μg of MNPs-Amx and spiked food samples containing L. monocytogenes (1.6 × 101–1.6 × 106 CFU/mL) were mixed in 1 mL of phosphate buffer saline (PBS, pH = 7.4, 10 mM) which contained 5 μL of 0.05% (w/v) PBST and incubated for 25 min at 37 °C, followed by magnetic separation for 4 min to obtain the MNPs-Amx ∼ L. monocytogenes complexes. Afterward, the complexes were dispersed in 1 mL of PBS with 0.2 mL of 1% (w/v) BSA and incubated for 30 min to block non-specific sites. After removing excess BSA through magnetic separation, the MNPs-Amx ∼ L. monocytogenes complexes were resuspended in 165 μL PBS, followed by the addition of 35 μL of Apt-GCDs-AgNCs and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C to obtain the sandwich complexes (MNPs-Amx ∼ L. monocytogenes ∼ Apt-GCDs-AgNCs). Afterward, the above complexes were washed twice by magnetic separation and resuspended in 200 μL PBS. Lastly, the fluorescence values of 180 μL of sandwich complex suspension were measured by a microplate reader at the optimal excitation wavelength (Ex) and emission wavelength (Em) of 395 nm and 625 nm, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial assay of prepared nanomaterials against L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: Eventually, after treating 104 and 106 CFU/mL of L. monocytogenes according to the procedure in section 2.1, the antimicrobial experiment was performed on the suspension to evaluate the antibacterial performance of GCDs-AgNCs. The experimental groups for the antimicrobial experiment were as follows: (1) L. monocytogenes (106 CFU/mL); (2) L. monocytogenes (106 CFU/mL) + GCDs; (3) L. monocytogenes (106 CFU/mL) + GCDs-AgNCs; (4) L. monocytogenes (104 CFU/mL); (5) L. monocytogenes (104 CFU/mL) + GCDs; (6) L. monocytogenes (104 CFU/mL) + GCDs-AgNCs. The final concentration of both GCDs and GCDs-AgNCs in the mixed solution was 20 μg/mL. The mixed solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 4 h and diluted to the appropriate bacterial concentration. Subsequently, 100 μL of the diluted solution was spread on LB agar plates and incubated at 37 °C for 16-18 h. Eventually, the antimicrobial activity of the prepared nanomaterials was determined by comparing the number of colonies between different groups.

All experiments were carried out in triple copies and the experimental results were calculated and analyzed by Microsoft Excel software. The processed experimental results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation (mean ± SD). Figures were plotted using PowerPoint software and Origin 9.0 software (OriginLab Corporation, Northampton, MA, USA). One-way ANOVA was used to compare the results of different experimental groups, and different lowercase letters indicated statistically significant differences (p < 0.05).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of fluorescence detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure for enrichment, fluorescence detection and elimination of L. monocytogenes is described in Scheme 1 . Amx is a typical β-lactam antibiotic that can recognize PBPs on the bacterial surface. Accordingly, we modified Amx on the surface of MNPs to synthesize MNPs-Amx for capturing L. monocytogenes in spiked food samples. Moreover, to realize the fluorescence detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes by this method, we synthesized GCDs with orange-red fluorescence and AgNCs with antimicrobial activity by hydrothermal and chemical reduction methods, respectively. Subsequently, bifunctional GCDs-AgNCs composites were constructed by the carbodiimide method. Eventually, L. monocytogenes-specific aptamers were modified on the surface of GCDs-AgNCs to form detection probes (Apt-GCDs-AgNCs) by the strong bonding force of the Au-S bond. On the one hand, fluorescence sensing of L. monocytogenes was realized by a sandwich detection strategy based on MNPs-Amx and Apt-GCDs-AgNCs, and the positive correlation between bacterial concentration and fluorescence value was constructed by measuring the fluorescence values at 395 nm and 625 nm of Ex and Em, respectively. On the other hand, AgNCs conferred excellent antimicrobial activity to GCDs-AgNCs, which enabled them to effectively eliminate L. monocytogenes from samples.

At first, the hydration particle size and zeta potential of MNPs-Amx were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS), respectively (Fig. 1 A). After modification with Amx, the average particle size of MNPs-Amx was increased from 219.7 to 250.4 nm. And the surface potentials were -23.3 and -25.4 mV before and after the modification, respectively. The change in potential was due to the occurrence of a carbodiimide reaction between the carboxyl group of MNPs and the amino group of Amx during the coupling process (Bai et al., 2022). Subsequently, the results of functional group characterization by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were shown in Fig. 1B. The absorption peak at 554 cm-1 was attributed to the Fe-O bond in the MNPs (Nemati et al., 2015), while the absorption peaks at 1065 cm-1 and 1451 cm-1 were the stretching vibration of the C-N-C and the C-C in Amx, respectively. The absorption peak at 1614 cm-1 represented the C=O stretching vibration in the amide bond (Bai et al., 2022). Eventually, the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of L. monocytogenes and MNPs-Amx ∼ L. monocytogenes (Fig. 1C) confirmed that the prepared MNPs-Amx was relatively uniform in particle size, well dispersed, and was capable of recognizing and capturing L. monocytogenes. These characterization results verified the successful synthesis of MNPs-Amx and the capture of L. monocytogenes by MNPs-Amx.

AgNCs were prepared by one-step chemical reduction method with AgNO3 as raw material, GSH as stabilizer and NaBH4 as reductant. The AgNCs were characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) and UV-vis absorption spectrum. The TEM image confirmed the synthesis of the quasi-spherical AgNCs (Fig. S1A), and the high-resolution TEM (HR TEM) image (Fig. S1B) displayed sharp lattice fringes with 0.233 nm lattice fringe distance, which were attributed to the diffraction plane of Ag (111) (Yang et al., 2022). The average size of AgNCs was subsequently calculated from the histogram of particle size distribution as 5.99 ± 0.96 nm (Fig. S1C). In addition, a characteristic absorbance peak at 425 nm was observed in the UV-vis absorption spectrum of AgNCs (Fig. S1D), which was attributed to the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect of AgNCs (Kalaivani et al., 2018). These results indicate the successful synthesis of AgNCs.

Subsequently, the morphology and chemical structure of the prepared GCDs were observed with TEM and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), respectively. The TEM images and the particle size distribution diagram (Fig. 2 A–C) showed that the GCDs were spheroidal with an average size of 1.72 ± 0.24 nm and lattice fringe distance of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the diffraction plane of graphitic carbon (100) and showed that the GCDs had a graphite-like structure (Ling et al., 2024). Moreover, the characteristic peaks of 531.0 eV, 400.0 eV, 284.8 eV, 162.9 eV and 84.0 eV in the XPS full spectra (Fig. 2D) belonged to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, S 2p and Au 4f, respectively, which indicated that the GCDs consisted of the elements of O, N, C, S and Au [with O: N: C: S: Au = 26.10: 14.85: 52.42: 4.87: 1.76 (Atomic%)]. HR XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 2E) indicated the existence of C-C/C=C (284.85 eV), C-O/C-N (286.09 eV), C-O (288.01 eV), and N-C-O (289.12 eV) bonds in GCDs. HR XPS O 1s (Fig. 2F) indicated the existence of C=O (531.40 eV) and C-O/C-OH (533.30 eV). HR XPS N 1s had characteristic peaks of pyridine N, pyrrole N, and graphite N at 398.52, 399.8, and 401.45 eV, respectively (Fig. 2G) (Pan et al., 2016). HR XPS Au 4f showed characteristic peaks at 87.67 and 84.00 eV for Au 4f5/2 and Au 4f7/2, respectively (Fig. 2H) (Yan, Chen, et al., 2022). The above results indicated that Au and S were doped in the GCDs.

Ultimately, the fluorescence properties of the prepared GCDs were thoroughly investigated. Fig. 2I revealed that the optimal Ex and Em of the GCDs were 395 nm and 625 nm, and the optimal Em exhibited an irrelevant property with the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2J). Fig. 2K showed that the GCDs maintained a relatively stable fluorescence signal with the extension of time. In the fluorescence decay curve in Fig. 2L, the fluorescence lifetime of the GCDs was calculated to be 2.605 μs under the 395 nm Ex. The above results indicate that the GCDs have superior fluorescence performances.

In addition, Fig. S2A proved that the coupled GCDs-AgNCs had a consistent fluorescence emission spectrum with GCDs. And the fluorescence signal was slightly decreased, which was probably due to a slight quenching effect of AgNCs. The GCDs-AgNCs were characterized by FT-IR spectra in Fig. S2B. The wide characteristic peak at 3266 cm-1 and the characteristic peak at 1522 cm-1 were respectively ascribed to the stretching vibrations of N-H/O-H and the bending vibration of N-H. The absorption peaks at 1630 cm-1 and 1232 cm-1 were ascribed to the stretching vibration of C=O and C-N in the amide bond (Liu et al., 2022), respectively, which indicated the successful conjugation of GCDs with AgNCs. Moreover, the characteristic peaks at 1035 cm-1, 630 cm-1 for GCDs and 1389 cm-1, 1593 cm-1 for AgNCs were retained in the conjugated GCDs-AgNCs, which further confirmed the successful synthesis of GCDs-AgNCs. In the end, the zeta potentials of Apt-GCDs-AgNCs were measured by using DLS (Fig. S2C). The surface potentials of GCDs, AgNCs, GCDs-AgNCs, and Apt-GCDs-AgNCs were -9.20 eV, -16.33 eV, -11.83 eV, and -21.57 eV, respectively. The changes in their surface potentials proved that the synthesis of GCDs-AgNCs and the successful modification of Apt.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial activity and mechanism of prepared AgNCs for L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: The antimicrobial efficacy of AgNCs was evaluated by measuring their minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) against L. monocytogenes (Fig. 3 A). The initial concentration of bacteria was 106 CFU/mL, and 20 μg/mL of AgNCs completely inhibited and eliminated the bacteria. And the MBC measurement of AgNCs was verified by the plate spreading method (Fig. 3C). Therefore, the MBC of AgNCs for L. monocytogenes was 20 μg/mL. Subsequently, the sterilization time of AgNCs was determined in Fig. 3B, and AgNCs can eliminate more than 99.99% of L. monocytogenes in samples within 4 h at the MBC. Bacterial biofilm can enhance the stress resistance of bacteria, thus the control and removal of bacterial biofilm is significant for food processing safety. The effect of AgNCs on L. monocytogenes biofilm was explored in Fig. S3, and the amount of L. monocytogenes biofilm was decreased with the increase of AgNCs concentration, which proved the effective removal of biofilm by AgNCs. However, under 4-fold MBC of AgNCs, L. monocytogenes still possessed approximately 10% biofilm compared to the control, indicating that biofilm can effectively improve bacterial resilience and tolerance to antimicrobial materials.

Afterward, the effect of AgNCs on the morphology of L. monocytogenes was observed by SEM images (Fig. S4). AgNCs can cause the cellular morphology of L. monocytogenes to crumple, and the degree of crumpling was correlated with the concentration of AgNCs. Moreover, the leakage of intracellular reducing sugar and protein of the bacteria was explored by DNS method and Coomassie brilliant blue method (Fig. S5). The intracellular reducing sugar and protein leakage of L. monocytogenes in the AgNCs-treated group was significantly increased compared with the control group. In addition, we investigated the effect of AgNCs on the metabolism of L. monocytogenes by measuring the intracellular ATP level and SDH enzyme activity. After being treated with AgNCs, the intracellular ATP amount of L. monocytogenes was reduced, and the corresponding SDH enzyme activity was significantly decreased (Fig. S6). These results indicated that AgNCs could eliminate L. monocytogenes by destroying cell structure and inhibiting energy metabolism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of detection procedure parameters for the proposed fluorescence detection strategy
ce:section.ce:para: The magnetic separation conditions and detection probe labeling conditions affecting the sensitivity of the fluorescent sensor for L. monocytogenes were optimized. On the one hand, the sensitivity of the constructed biosensor was significantly affected by the amount of L. monocytogenes obtained by magnetic separation, thus the optimization experiments were performed on the MNPs-Amx dosage, incubation time, and magnetic separation time. The CE of MNPs-Amx against L. monocytogenes was maximized and maintained at the dosage of 40 μg of MNP-Amx (Fig. 4 A), incubation time of 25 min (Fig. 4B), and magnetic separation time of 4 min (Fig. 4C). Therefore, the above optimized magnetic separation parameters were used in subsequent experiments. On the other hand, the labeling of the detection probe was directly related to the fluorescence signal of this biosensor, and a weaker fluorescence signal would result in a narrower linear quantification range with a lower signal-to-noise ratio. When the volume of Apt-GCDs-AgNCs was 35 μL (Fig. 4D) and the incubation time was 30 min (Fig. 4E), the biosensor achieved the optimal detection sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Capability and stability of MNPs-Amx to separate L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: As PBPs are widely present on the surface of various bacteria, the broad-spectrum separation property of MNPs-Amx for bacteria was explored by selecting and measuring the CEs of MNPs-Amx for L. monocytogenes, B. cereus, S. aureus, S. typhimurium, E. coli O157:H7, and S. flexneri. The results showed that the CEs for S. aureus, B. cereus, and L. monocytogenes were more than 95%, while the CEs of S. typhimurium, S. flexneri, and E. coli O157:H7 were less than 10% (Fig. S7A). This might be due to the different amounts of PBPs in different bacteria or the different affinity of Amx with different types of PBPs on the surface of bacteria (Georgopapadakou & Liu, 1980). Moreover, since contaminated real samples typically contained multiple bacteria, the interference of non-target bacteria was required to be considered during the separation of target bacteria. The experimental results showed that the CE of MNPs-Amx was around 90% for target bacteria (104 or 103 CFU/mL) at the same or low concentration in the mixed bacteria (Fig. S7B). Meanwhile, the separation performance of MNPs-Amx for L. monocytogenes in spiked samples was further verified under the optimized magnetic separation parameters. As seen in Fig. S7C, the CE of MNPs-Amx for L. monocytogenes (101–106 CFU/mL) in spiked samples was more than 84.53%, which indicated its applicability in real samples. Eventually, the effects of salt concentration and different pH on capturing L. monocytogenes by MNPs-Amx were investigated to demonstrate the stability of the capture performance of MNPs-Amx in foods with different salt contents or different pH. As seen in Fig. S8, the CE of MNPs-Amx against L. monocytogenes was more than 90% at different NaCl concentrations (100 ∼ 500 mM) or different pH (4 ∼ 9). These above results showed that MNPs-Amx had great capture capability and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity of the constructed fluorescent biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the proposed fluorescence detection strategy for L. monocytogenes, six common bacteria were detected with the proposed method. As seen in Fig. 5 , the fluorescence signal of the non-target bacteria assay was comparable to the control, whereas obvious fluorescence signals were observed in the detection of target bacteria or mixed bacteria (including L. monocytogenes and others), demonstrating that the successful modification of Apt realized the specificity of the detection strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of the constructed fluorescence biosensor for detecting L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: According to the constructed fluorescence detection strategy (Fig. 4F), we explored the applicability of the constructed fluorescent biosensor in spiked samples under the optimized experimental parameters. As shown in Fig. 6 , the fluorescence signal of the detection gradually increased as the concentration of L. monocytogenes increased from 1.6 × 101 to 1.6 × 106 CFU/mL. Standard curves were constructed to quantify the target bacteria (L. monocytogenes) in spiked samples, and the fitted linear equations were as follows: y = 0.12733x + 0.25199 (R2 = 0.99129, PBS), y = 0.1374x + 0.19368 (R2 = 0.99137, spiked lettuce) and y = 0.1367x + 0.18614 (R2 = 0.99517, spiked juice). The LOD was 160 CFU/mL in all samples. The above results indicate that the established fluorescent biosensor has high detection sensitivity and wide detection range, attributed to the good separation and enrichment of bacteria by MNP-Amx and good recognition of target bacteria by Apt-GCDs-AgNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the antimicrobial effect of GCDs-AgNCs against L. monocytogenes
ce:section.ce:para: Eventually, after detecting L. monocytogenes using the constructed fluorescent biosensor, the elimination ability of GCDs and GCDs-AgNCs for L. monocytogenes at the same concentration was explored. As shown in Fig. S9, after 4 h of co-incubation, GCDs had essentially no antimicrobial effect against L. monocytogenes at low concentration (104 CFU/mL) and high concentration (106 CFU/mL). After coupling AgNCs to form GCDs-AgNCs, the nanomaterials could eliminate L. monocytogenes at both low and high concentrations in the samples, indicating that the antibacterial effect of AgNCs was retained in the GCDs-AgNCs composites. Combined with the fluorescence property of GCDs-AgNCs, the composites were bifunctional in fluorescence detection and bacterial elimination. Moreover, compared with other methods, the synthesis process of nanomaterials in the proposed method is simpler, the antimicrobial treatment is more direct, and the detection signals are obtained more sensitively and quickly (Robby et al., 2021).

In conclusion, we designed a bifunctional nanomaterial (GCDs-AgNCs) for sensitive detection and effective elimination of L. monocytogenes from samples. The specific detection of L. monocytogenes was achieved by enriching L. monocytogenes with MNPs-Amx and labeling with Apt-GCDs-AgNCs probe. The LOD was as low as 160 CFU/mL, and the entire detection process consumed approximately 110 min. In addition, antimicrobial experiments indicated that the GCDs-AgNCs retained the antimicrobial performance of AgNCs, which could eliminate practically all L. monocytogenes in the samples through the action mechanisms of destroying the cell structure of bacteria and affecting bacterial metabolism. Therefore, the bifunctional properties of the prepared GCDs-AgNCs could provide some valuable references for the quantitative analysis and effective elimination of bacteria, thus promoting further exploration in the synthesis and application of multifunctional nanomaterials. Further research could focus on exploring other antibacterial mechanisms of AgNCs or enhancing the fluorescence intensity of GCDs-AgNCs composites in certain methods to improve the detection sensitivity of the fluorescent biosensor.

Zhiming Ling: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Qian Xu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Weiqiang Li: Formal analysis, Data curation. Jiatian Gao: Software. Hengyi Xu: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful to the Research Project of State Key Laboratory of Food Science and Resources, Nanchang University (Project No. SKLF-ZZB-202328) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para:
